one_line_takeaway: A compact black-box benchmark showing the GPT family is broadly fragile to trivial character-level typos in classification prompts and that jailbreak resistance scales sharply with model generation (GPT-4o 95.7% vs GPT-3.5-turbo 48.9%).
key_finding: All four models suffer large accuracy drops under trivial character-level perturbation. GPT-4o has the highest clean accuracy but also the largest absolute drop; GPT-3.5-turbo has lower clean accuracy but proportionally smaller degradation, suggesting a baseline-performance vs. robustness trade-off. SST-2 is the hardest dataset (largest drop). For jailbreak resistance, newer/larger models are far more robust: GPT-4o resists 95.7% of jailbreak prompts while GPT-3.5-turbo resists only 48.9%, a stark safety-mechanism gap across the family.
core_contribution: An empirical robustness evaluation of four proprietary GPT-family models (GPT-4o, GPT-4, GPT-4-turbo, GPT-3.5-turbo) against two prompt-level adversarial threats: (1) character-level text perturbations applied to sentiment-classification inputs, and (2) in-the-wild jailbreak prompts targeting safety mechanisms. The paper's own contribution is the comparative measurement across these four models on both axes, not the attack techniques or datasets themselves, which are drawn from prior work (character-level attacks credited to HotFlip [13]; the 1405 jailbreak prompts taken directly from the JailbreakHub dataset of Shen et al. [14]). The authors position their novelty as filling a gap in evaluating the GPT family specifically under prompt-based perturbations.
threat_model: Adversary controls the text of the input prompt sent to a deployed LLM. Two attacker goals: (a) integrity/availability degradation via realistic typo-like character deletions (randomly deleting one character per word) that cause misclassification without changing apparent meaning to a human; and (b) safety-policy circumvention via crafted jailbreak prompts (role-play framing, prompt injection, deceptive formatting) that induce the model to produce content it would normally refuse. No model internals, gradients, or weights are assumed (black-box, input-only access). No defense is proposed; the work is purely an attack/vulnerability assessment.
limitations: Evaluated only closed GPT models from a single vendor (no open-weight or non-OpenAI models), so conclusions do not generalize across the LLM landscape. The jailbreak 'detection' metric is under-specified: it is unclear whether it measures refusal, a classifier flag, or harmful-output presence, and no harmful target queries or human evaluation of outputs are described, making the 95.7%/48.9% figures hard to reproduce. The character-level attack is a single weak, non-adaptive random-deletion scheme at one fixed configuration (no sweep over Pd/Nmax, no comparison to stronger word- or sentence-level attacks despite citing them). Sample sizes per sentiment dataset are not reported. No defense, adversarial training, or mitigation is actually implemented despite being called for. Table II is mislabeled 'Table 1' in the body text, and the paper is short (workshop-scale) with limited statistical analysis (no confidence intervals or significance tests).

datasets_used: SST-2 / Stanford Sentiment Treebank (fine-grained movie-review sentiment)
JailbreakHub jailbreak-prompt collection (1405 jailbreak prompts curated from 15,140 total, Dec 2022-Dec 2023) [from Shen et al. 2024]
quantitative_results: GPT-4o IMDB accuracy: 96.4% clean -> 79.3% under character attack
GPT-4o SST-2 accuracy: 90.3% -> 70.9%; GPT-4 88.7% -> 68.7%; GPT-4-turbo 88.9% -> 69.4%; GPT-3.5-turbo 86.9% -> 62.2% (largest single drop)
Jailbreak detection/resistance on 1405 prompts: GPT-4o 1344/1405 = 95.7%; GPT-4-turbo 1284/1405 = 91.4%; GPT-4 1263/1405 = 89.9%; GPT-3.5-turbo 687/1405 = 48.9%
Character-attack configuration: 1 char altered per word, Pd = 0.05, Nmax = 10 alterations per sentence
baselines_compared: Clean (unperturbed) prompt accuracy serves as the within-model baseline for the character-level attack
Cross-model comparison among GPT-4o, GPT-4, GPT-4-turbo, and GPT-3.5-turbo (no external/third-party defense or model baselines)
